description:
You are a performance engineer who has just been given a pair of micro-benchmarks that already exist on the system.  
Your goal is to run them **concurrently**, capture their individual results, and then prepare a summarized CSV file that a CI pipeline will later ingest.

SYSTEM LAYOUT (already present):
1. Benchmark directory:  
   /home/user/perf_bench/apps
2. Two executable files inside that directory:  
   a) /home/user/perf_bench/apps/app_sort.py  
   b) /home/user/perf_bench/apps/app_math.py  

Each of those Python scripts is self-contained, executable, and when launched with no arguments prints exactly **one** line to STDOUT following this pattern (the number is always the same for a given app):

benchmark_result_ms:<integer>

For example, the sort benchmark prints  
benchmark_result_ms:42  
and the math benchmark prints  
benchmark_result_ms:84  

WHAT YOU MUST DO:
Step-1  Launch both benchmarks **in parallel** from the directory /home/user/perf_bench/apps.  
        • Make sure that each benchmark’s STDOUT goes to a dedicated, app-specific log file:  
          /home/user/perf_bench/logs/app_sort.log  
          /home/user/perf_bench/logs/app_math.log  
        • Use background execution (or any other form of parallelism) so that both benchmarks run at the same time.  
        • Wait for both to finish before continuing.

Step-2  After both processes complete, parse the two log files and create a new directory if it does not exist:  
        /home/user/perf_bench/summary

Step-3  Write a CSV file named /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_summary.csv with the following exact requirements:  
        • First row is the header:  
          app_name,run_datetime,result_ms  
        • Each subsequent row corresponds to one benchmark.  
        • app_name should be app_sort or app_math.  
        • run_datetime must be the ISO-8601 local date-time of when **that individual benchmark ended**, with seconds resolution, formatted YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS (no timezone suffix).  
        • result_ms is the integer number extracted from benchmark_result_ms:<integer>.  
        • Rows must be ordered alphabetically by app_name (app_math after app_sort).

        Example of correct CSV layout (timestamps will differ):
        app_name,run_datetime,result_ms
        app_math,2024-06-14T10:13:59,84
        app_sort,2024-06-14T10:14:00,42

Step-4  Create a human-readable report named /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_report.md with exactly the following structure:

        # Benchmark Report
        Generated: <ISO-8601 timestamp of when you generated this file>

        | App | Result (ms) |
        |-----|-------------|
        | app_math | 84 |
        | app_sort | 42 |

        All wording and table borders must match precisely (except for the “Generated:” timestamp).

VERIFICATION NOTES:
• The automated test will verify that the two log files exist and contain the unmodified benchmark output line.  
• It will then read /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_summary.csv and check:
  - correct header
  - exactly two rows, alphabetically ordered
  - correct integer results
  - each run_datetime is syntactically valid ISO-8601 with seconds (regex: ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}$)
• It will finally open /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_report.md and validate that:
  - The first line is “# Benchmark Report”
  - The second line starts with “Generated: ”
  - The markdown table has the exact six lines (header separator line included) with the values shown above.

Make sure to:
• Keep every file path **exactly** as specified (case-sensitive).  
• Do **not** modify the contents of the benchmark scripts; only launch them.  
• Do not hard-code timestamps; they must be collected dynamically.  
• Use only standard GNU/Linux user-level tools available in a minimal distribution.

When you are done you should have exactly four new or updated files:
1. /home/user/perf_bench/logs/app_sort.log
2. /home/user/perf_bench/logs/app_math.log
3. /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_summary.csv
4. /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_report.md
truth:
Directory /home/user/perf_bench/apps already exists with the two files:

/home/user/perf_bench/apps/app_sort.py  (mode 0755)
----------------------------------------------------
#!/usr/bin/env python3
print("benchmark_result_ms:42")

/home/user/perf_bench/apps/app_math.py  (mode 0755)
----------------------------------------------------
#!/usr/bin/env python3
print("benchmark_result_ms:84")

The testing framework will look for:

1. /home/user/perf_bench/logs/app_sort.log  
   Must contain exactly one line:  
   benchmark_result_ms:42

2. /home/user/perf_bench/logs/app_math.log  
   Must contain exactly one line:  
   benchmark_result_ms:84

3. /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_summary.csv  
   Must match:  
   app_name,run_datetime,result_ms\n
   app_math,<ANY_VALID_ISO_8601_DATETIME>,84\n
   app_sort,<ANY_VALID_ISO_8601_DATETIME>,42\n

4. /home/user/perf_bench/summary/perf_report.md  
   First six non-empty lines must be:
   # Benchmark Report
   Generated: <ANY_TEXT>
   | App | Result (ms) |
   |-----|-------------|
   | app_math | 84 |
   | app_sort | 42 |

Both "<ANY_VALID_ISO_8601_DATETIME>" and "<ANY_TEXT>" are placeholders the grader will match using regex.

All other paths, names and contents must remain unchanged.
